narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile like your solid LSAT score and upper-middle family background. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors from past students, estimated a 66.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam—high enough to lead to the rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your school decile rankings from earlier years, which stood out as the biggest concerns compared to typical applicants. Specifically, your Year 3 decile of 1 was well below the average of around 5.5, strongly pushing the model toward expecting bar failure because lower rankings there have historically correlated with tougher outcomes on the exam. Your Year 1 decile of 4, also under the typical 5.7 average, added to this concern as the second-most influential factor pulling in the same direction. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 36—close to the dataset average of 36.7—helped counter this by nudging the prediction toward success, as did your undergraduate GPA of 3.1, which is only slightly below the 3.2 norm. Your upper-middle family income and full-time student status played smaller roles, with the income level offering a modest positive influence while full-time enrollment had little impact. Overall, your early academic deciles were lower than what the model sees in students who typically pass the bar, setting you apart from the stronger profiles that get approved. While this is disappointing, especially given the barriers many Black women face in higher education, it's not the end of your journey. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your academic deciles through targeted study habits or additional coursework, perhaps retaking exams or seeking mentorship programs designed for underrepresented students. You might also consider part-time options or building a standout personal statement highlighting your resilience. Keep pushing forward—many successful lawyers have overcome similar hurdles, and with these adjustments, you can improve your odds significantly. We're rooting for you.
